g.data-narration: Welcome to Collection Algorithms! Learn how Java provides powerful built-in methods for sorting, searching, and manipulating collections efficiently.
Collection Algorithms are static utility methods in the Collections class that perform common operations like sorting, searching, and shuffling on any collection type.
g.text: Static utility methods in the Collections class that operate on collections:

g.data-narration: These algorithms are polymorphic, efficient, type-safe with generics, and provide convenient ready-to-use solutions for common data manipulation tasks.
g.text: O(n log n), O(log n) complexity
No need to write custom code

g.data-narration: Collections.sort() uses modified mergesort offering O(n log n) performance. It sorts lists in natural order or with a custom Comparator for specific sorting rules.
g.text: Collections.sort() uses modified mergesort - O(n log n) time complexity

g.data-narration: Binary search requires a sorted list and works by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half, achieving O(log n) time complexity for fast lookups.
g.text: Collections.binarySearch() requires SORTED list - O(log n) time complexity

g.data-narration: Binary search compares the target with the middle element. If equal, return index. If target is smaller, search the left half; otherwise search the right half.
g.text: If not found after exhaustive search: returns -(insertionPoint) - 1

g.data-narration: Collections.shuffle() randomly permutes list elements. Collections.min() and max() find extreme values in O(n) time using natural ordering or a Comparator.
Other useful algorithms: frequency() counts occurrences, disjoint() checks for common elements, copy() duplicates lists, and fill() replaces all elements with a value.
g.text: More Algorithms: Frequency, Disjoint, Copy, Fill

g.data-narration: The Arrays class provides similar algorithms for arrays: sort, binarySearch, equals, toString, fill, and asList for converting arrays to fixed-size lists.
g.text: Static methods for manipulating arrays (similar to Collections but for arrays)

g.data-narration: Arrays.asList() creates a fixed-size list backed by the original array. Modifications affect both, but add/remove operations throw UnsupportedOperationException.
g.text: Fixed-size list backed by array - set() works, add()/remove() fail
Arrays.deepEquals() and deepToString() for multi-dimensional arrays

g.data-narration: Understanding time complexity helps choose the right algorithm: sort is O(n log n), binary search is O(log n), shuffle and min/max are O(n).
Real-world example: A student grade management system using Collections.sort() with Comparable and Comparator for custom sorting by grade or name.
g.text: int grade; // natural sort by grade

g.data-narration: Remember: Collections.sort() for Lists, Arrays.sort() for arrays. Binary search requires sorted data. Arrays.asList() creates fixed-size lists. Know your time complexities!
g.text: Master these algorithms for efficient Java data manipulation!

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Remember class names (Collections vs Arrays), binary search requires sorted data, know time complexities, and practice custom Comparators.

g.text: Remember class names: Collections for collections, Arrays for arrays
Binary search: always sort first, or results are undefined
Time complexity: sort O(n log n), binary search O(log n), min/max O(n)
Arrays.asList() returns fixed-size list - can't add/remove elements
